did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a hybrid deep learning model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and VGG19 for the early detection and classification of skin cancer and oral cancer. It reports achieving 97% accuracy, 93% recall, 92% F1-score, and 92% precision, and compares this proposed model against existing algorithms such as AlexNet, ResNet-50, and Inception-V3.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, specifically proposing a novel hybrid deep learning model (CNN and VGG19) for skin and oral cancer detection. It reports new empirical results (97% accuracy, 93% recall, etc.) obtained from training and testing on the ISIC 2019 dataset, and compares these new results against existing algorithms (AlexNet, ResNet-50, Inception-V3). Although it contains a literature review section, its primary contribution is the development and validation of a new diagnostic algorithm, classifying it as a research paper.

title: The Detection of Skin Cancer and Oral Cancer : A comparison of the proposed Hybrid Model with the Existing Detection Algorithms
journal: 2023 Fifth International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Communication Technologies (ICECCT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and VGG19 for the early detection and classification of skin and oral cancers. The model was trained and tested using the ISIC 2019 dataset, which includes skin lesion photos and an online-collected dataset for oral cancer. The authors evaluated the proposed model's performance against existing state-of-the-art algorithms, specifically AlexNet, ResNet-50, and Inception-V3. The proposed hybrid model achieved an accuracy of 97%, recall of 93%, F1-score of 92%, and precision of 92%. These results indicate that the proposed model outperforms the compared existing classifiers in identifying skin and oral cancer types.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the primary skin cancer classes studied in the ISIC 2019 dataset experiments. The paper reports 2099 total images, with 1901 correctly identified by the proposed model.

One of the primary skin cancer classes studied in the ISIC 2019 dataset experiments. The paper reports 622 total images, with 58 misclassified as melanoma, 45 as pigmented benign keratosis, and 32 as vascular lesion.
One of the primary skin lesion classes studied in the ISIC 2019 dataset experiments. The paper reports 1782 total images, with 1480 correctly identified by the proposed model.
One of the primary skin lesion classes studied in the ISIC 2019 dataset experiments. The paper reports 573 total images, with 492 correctly identified by the proposed model.

One of the primary disease categories studied in the ISIC 2019 dataset experiments. The paper reports 3634 photos from oral cancer patients with squamous cell carcinoma, all accurately diagnosed by the proposed model.

paper_terms: normal images devoid of any form of cancer
lesion_types_studied.details: Mentioned as a category in the experimental results section where images are categorized into carcinogenic skin types or normal images.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin and oral cancer (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Vascular Lesion, Oral Cancer/Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Normal/Non-cancerous skin).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model is for 'classification of skin and oral cancer' and 'categorised as having carcinogenic skin'. It reports performance metrics (Accuracy 97%, Recall 93%, F1-score 92%, Precision 92%) typical of classification tasks. The model predicts discrete classes from the ISIC 2019 dataset, including multi-class skin lesion types and oral cancer presence.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing a large number of skin lesion photos and an online-collected dataset for oral cancer, used for the training and testing of the proposed hybrid model.

use.training: Used for training the proposed hybrid CNN and VGG19 model.
use.validation: Used for validation (split ratio 7:2:1, with validation coming last).
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed model and comparing it against AlexNet, ResNet-50, and Inception-V3.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed hybrid model and existing algorithms (AlexNet, ResNet-50, Inception-V3) were based on descriptive metrics (Accuracy, Recall, F1-score, Precision) without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC 2019 dataset.notes: The proposed model outperformed existing algorithms. Specific classification counts for the proposed model included: 1901 correctly identified Melanoma images (out of 2099 total, with 75 misclassified as pigmented benign keratosis, 56 as vascular lesion, and 67 as BCC); 58 Basal Cell Carcinoma images misclassified as melanoma, 45 as pigmented benign keratosis, and 32 as vascular lesion (out of 622 total); 1480 correctly identified Pigmented Benign Keratosis images (out of 1782 total, with 130 misclassified as melanoma, 78 as vascular lesion, and 94 as BCC); 492 correctly identified Vascular Lesion images (out of 573 total, with 21 misclassified as melanoma, 29 as pigmented benign keratosis, and 31 as BCC); and 3634 Oral Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma) images accurately diagnosed.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid model combining Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and VGG19

components: CNN consisting of a convolution layer, a fully-convolutional layer, and a pooling layer
architecture_details.feature_map_formula: FM = FS * I(m)[x, y] = sum(sum(i, j) FS(i, j) I(m)[x + i, y + j])
architecture_details.padding: Zero padding used to keep edges of output image and input image same; ZP = (f(width) - 1)/2

training_details.preprocessing: Keras ImageData Generator used; images scaled down from original 450x600 pixels to 224x224 pixels; data normalization performed

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images scaled down from original dimensions of 450x600 pixels to 224x224 pixels using Keras ImageData Generator.
Normalization: Data normalization performed before providing data for training to determine if the model was trained correctly.

Noise reduction: Mentioned as a general preprocessing approach used in related literature (e.g., Nawaz et al.), but specific techniques for the proposed model are limited to scaling and normalization.
conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model, combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and VGG19, achieved an accuracy of 97%, recall of 93%, F1-score of 92%, and precision of 92% for the detection of skin and oral cancer.
The proposed hybrid model outperformed existing state-of-the-art algorithms, specifically ResNet-50, AlexNet, and Inception-V3, in terms of accuracy, recall, F1-score, and precision.

The model demonstrated high performance in classifying specific skin lesion types, correctly identifying 1901 out of 2099 melanoma images, 1480 out of 1782 pigmented benign keratosis images, and 492 out of 573 vascular lesion images.
The proposed model accurately diagnosed all 3634 images of oral cancer (squamous cell carcinoma) from the dataset.
Among the compared algorithms, Inception-V3 produced the poorest outcomes for the identification of skin and oral cancer.
important_remarks: The study is a conference paper (ICECCT 2023) with limited scope for rigorous clinical validation compared to journal publications.

The dataset combines skin lesion images from the public ISIC 2019 dataset with an 'online-collected dataset' for oral cancer; the provenance, size, and quality of the oral cancer subset are not fully detailed, raising concerns about data heterogeneity and potential bias.
No data augmentation techniques were employed for the proposed model, which may limit generalizability given the relatively small sample sizes for some classes (e.g., 622 BCC images).

The paper reports no statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the proposed model and baseline algorithms (AlexNet, ResNet-50, Inception-V3).

The data split ratio is described as 7:2:1 (Training:Testing:Validation) in the text, which is an unconventional ordering and lacks standard cross-validation or independent external test set evaluation.

The model achieves 100% accuracy on the oral cancer class (3634 images), which is unusually high for medical imaging tasks and may indicate data leakage, class imbalance, or lack of challenging negative controls within that specific subset.
There is no mention of external validation on independent datasets or clinical deployment trials, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical relevance.

The comparison against state-of-the-art models (ResNet-50, Inception-V3) uses standard metrics but does not account for potential differences in preprocessing, hyperparameter tuning, or training protocols across the compared models.
